title: Hodge-Theoretic Anabelian Geometry
speaker: Qixiang Wang
abstract:
The anabelian phenomenon may be viewed as an arithmetic analogue of Mostow rigidity: it predicts that certain varieties can be reconstructed from their arithmetic fundamental groups. A celebrated result of S. Mochizuki shows that hyperbolic curves over p-adic fields exhibit this phenomenon. 
Inspired by non-abelian Hodge theory, we introduce a Hodge-theoretic analogue of arithmetic fundamental groups for complex Kähler manifolds, and show that an anabelian phenomenon occurs in complex-analytic geometry. In particular, hyperbolic Riemann surfaces are uniquely determined by their Hodge-theoretic fundamental groups, yielding a complex-analytic analogue of Mochizuki’s result. If time permits, we will discuss higher-dimensional generalizations.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Anabelian rigidity principle
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates anabelian geometry from rigidity phenomena and formulates Grothendieck’s idea that, for suitable K(pi,1)-like varieties, maps of varieties should be recoverable from outer homomorphisms of fundamental groups.

proof_stages.label: Calibration by basic nonexamples and conjectural examples
proof_stages.summary: The definition is tested on standard algebraic objects, showing that P1, elliptic curves, abelian varieties, and moduli of abelian varieties fail the anabelian condition while moduli of curves are expected to satisfy it.

proof_stages.label: Mochizuki theorem as Hodge-theoretic target
proof_stages.summary: The central arithmetic benchmark is Mochizuki’s theorem that hyperbolic curves over number fields or p-adic fields are anabelian for dominant maps/open homomorphisms, whose proof uses p-adic Hodge theory and motivates a modern Hodge-theoretic reformulation.

proof_stages.label: Complex replacement of the arithmetic Galois group
proof_stages.summary: To make a complex-analytic analogue, the missing Galois group is replaced by a C* action arising from nonabelian Hodge theory on local systems/Higgs bundles, and center-freeness allows one to work with outer actions on pro-algebraic completions rather than an actual arithmetic fundamental group extension.

proof_stages.label: C*-equivariant anabelian statement
proof_stages.summary: The relevant homomorphisms of fundamental groups are defined to be those whose induced maps on pro-algebraic completions are C*-equivariant, and the complex theorem is stated for hyperbolic curves, with comments on its interpretation as preservation of complex variations of Hodge structure and on broader variants.

proof_stages.label: Uniformizing local systems and Hitchin fixed points
proof_stages.summary: For a hyperbolic curve, its upper-half-plane uniformization gives a faithful discrete PSL2(R) local system lying in Teichmuller space, and via the nonabelian Hodge correspondence the Hitchin fibration/section identifies the uniformizing point as the C*-fixed zero-fiber point.

proof_stages.label: Pullback rigidity and reconstruction of the curve
proof_stages.summary: A C*-equivariant isomorphism of fundamental groups pulls back the uniformizing local system of one curve to a polarized variation of Hodge structure on the other, forcing it by the Hitchin fixed-point argument to be the other uniformizing local system and hence making the upper-half-plane actions compatible, so the curves are isomorphic.

examples.name: Mostow rigidity for hyperbolic manifolds
examples.note: Mostow rigidity is used as the motivating paradigm: the fundamental group of a hyperbolic manifold determines its geometric structure, suggesting an analogous reconstruction principle in algebraic geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites Mostow rigidity as an inspiring theorem and states its conclusion, but does not work through an example or proof.

passes.reason: Hyperbolic manifolds are locally modeled on maximally symmetric constant-negative-curvature space, and Mostow rigidity is used as a motivating reconstruction theorem rather than a failure case.

examples.name: The projective line
examples.note: The projective line is used as the most basic algebraic variety and as an immediate non-anabelian case because its geometric fundamental group is trivial.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker tests whether the projective line is anabelian by using its trivial geometric fundamental group to conclude it is not.

passes.reason: The projective line is the genus-zero/minimal curve with trivial geometric fundamental group and large PGL2 symmetry, and it is cited as not anabelian.

examples.name: Elliptic curves
examples.note: Elliptic curves illustrate that being a K(pi,1) is not enough for anabelianity, since their geometric fundamental group/Tate module gives too many group-theoretic automorphisms compared with geometric automorphisms.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes elliptic curves via the exact sequence of fundamental groups, Tate modules, automorphism groups, and the Kummer map to show the anabelian condition fails.

passes.reason: Elliptic curves are the genus-one boundary case with abelian Tate-module fundamental group, and they are used to show the anabelian condition fails despite being K(pi,1).
Elliptic curves are the genus-one boundary case with abelian fundamental group, used to show the anabelian Hom comparison fails.

examples.note: Abelian varieties, and then their moduli spaces, are presented as further non-anabelian objects where large centers in the relevant profinite symplectic groups lead to group-theoretic automorphisms not matched geometrically.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the same style of group-theoretic argument to abelian varieties and works out for A_g how the profinite symplectic fundamental group and its large center force too many automorphisms.
